questions.text: I find that the moral pull of suffering is what keeps me honest about my priorities.
I set up my environment to minimize distractions before starting focused work.
I tend to re-feel past emotional experiences at nearly their original intensity when I remember them.
I track my progress methodically to ensure I'm meeting my targets.
When there is a vacuum of leadership, I fill it without thinking twice.
I hold the view that people who communicate indirectly create unnecessary problems.
I notice the social signals that indicate someone is ready to end a conversation.
I believe that my confidence grows when I consistently invest in myself.
I find myself tracking the emotional trajectory of a conversation as it unfolds.
When facing a decision, I often go with my first instinct rather than deliberating at length.
I think that structured reasoning about distressing circumstances is a discipline that protects against the kind of impulsive action that can create new problems.
When I get frustrated, the frustration escalates rapidly to a peak.
I am drawn to roles where I can shape outcomes directly.
I prefer joint ownership of outcomes so that no single person bears the weight.
I find that the felt weight of suffering is the moral datum that I return to again and again when I need to check my reasoning.
I believe that my emotional steadiness is a constitutional trait, not something I have to work at.
I feel that personal boundaries should be flexible when someone truly needs my help.
I would rather go without something I want so that someone else can have what they need.
